pdf_upgrd_description: Earrings, gold. Pair of identical gold earrings with rings on both sides, small pieces attached to the rings by a small fragrance belt, and four small rings at the ends, with hanging elements shaped like bells.

Human statue, stone, fragmentary, with broken limbs. The figure is shown wearing a robe extending the full length of the body. The face is clearly defined, with a long nose, beard, two wandering eyebrows, and two eyes with jealous pupils. The description is incomplete in the source text.
Sculptural fragments, bronze and marble stone: two pieces comprising the front of a bronze right foot and a marble stone hand. Third century BC. Dimensions: 6.5 cm and 6 cm.
Lot, art, dimensions 12.7 × 18.5.
Jar, burned clay, with a slightly wide outward-flaring nozzle, puffed body, side handles, and decoration of geometric crawling triangles and by-lines painted in black and red.
Object, material not specified. No descriptive information about the object is provided beyond the text “9 - 16 1.66 LOT - ART - 16 1.66 LOT - ART.”

Head of a human statue, marble, depicting a male head with a long neck and a partially preserved face. The eyes are indicated by drilled pupils, the nose is pierced, the eyebrows are arched and joined to the nose, the mouth is pierced, and the ears are preserved on both sides.

Animal statue, bronze, depicting a camel with a hard head, a slight mid-back hump, and a strapped small tail behind the hind legs. Metal. Dimensions: 10.2 cm x 9 cm (4 x 3.55 in); additional measurement given as 3.9 in (9.9 cm). Early millennium BCE.
Lot, material not specified. No further object description is provided in the source text.
Pavilion, bronze, with a nine-line inscription and a composition featuring the stylized forms of a recumbent animal mediated by a palm tree; two holes are present in the upper corners.

Object type not specified; material not specified. The source text contains only the fragment “6 . 1.1 19.5 LOT - ART . 1.1 19.5 LOT - ART” and does not provide sufficient information to identify the object, its materials, typology, technique, dimensions, or period.
Human doll, material not specified. A human doll, recorded as lot 7:1-2-15.8×9×0, with dimensions 15.8 × 9 × 0.
Object, material not specified, with recorded dimensions of 17.4, 13.9, 11.8, 11.3, 11.3, 7.5, and 8 cm.
Coin, metal, old Yemeni coin with an owl bird depicted on one face and what appears to be a human face on the other; dated to about the third century BCE. Weight 4.93 g, diameter 15 mm.
Coin, metal. Old Yemeni coin with human face motifs on both sides, one face bearded and the other also bearded. Dated to the second century and the first century BCE. Weight 1.88 g; diameter 11 mm.

Coin, metal, an old Yemeni coin with one face bearing a human face in relief, apparently wearing an earring. Dated to around the middle of the 4th century to the 3rd century BCE. Dimensions: 11 mm; weight: 1.98 g.

Coin, metal. Old Yemeni coin with one face bearing an expression of a human face with clear features, apparently wearing an earring. Dated to around the middle of the 4th century to the 3rd century BCE. Diameter 12 mm.

Coin, metal. Old Yemeni coin with one face bearing a human face in relief with clear features, apparently wearing an earring. Dated to around the middle of the 4th century to the 3rd century BCE. Diameter 12 mm.

Coin, metal. Old Yemeni coin with one face bearing an expression of a human face, with a clear glimpse, apparently wearing an earring. Dated to around the middle of the 4th century and the 3rd century BCE. Diameter: 12 mm.

Bronze plaque, with a prominent inscribed frame and a nine-line inscription in barge-line format, measuring 10.2 × 14.4 cm; the text is recorded as beginning with “1-A-L-A-T-A-T-A-D-Y/Y-A-N-E-N-E-E-R-E-R-E-R-N-E-R-N-E-R-N-E-N-E-N-E-N-A-N-A-T-E-N-E-T-E-N-E-N-E-N-E-N-E-N-E-N-E-N-E-N-N-E-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-N-E-N-N-N-E-N-N-E-N-N-E-N-E-E-N-E-E-E-N-N-E-E-”; dated broadly to the first millennium BCE to the early first millennium CE.
Animal statue, material not specified. An animal statue.

Gravestones, marble and stone: a group of gravestones including examples with human heads carved in marble, one bearing an unclear inscription, probably the name of the deceased, together with two additional fragments, one in marble and one in stone.

Human gravestones, marble stone, of different sizes, including a group of gravestones with human heads. Three examples are heavily fragmentary, preserving long, prominent noses, two broken eyes, and a small mouth, with most of their parts lost.

Gravestone, stone. Human tombstone with a carved face in relief, showing a high nose, long pointed features, blind eyes pierced through the eye area, a light smile, a slightly shorter nose, and two eyebrows.

Animal statue, bronze, depicting a camel with a high head and two prominent ears, the neck encircled by a collar, a sleeve on the back, and the tail held close to the hind legs; an inscription on the right side appears to be in aristocratic script but is unclear.

Animal head, bronze. Head fragment of an animal statue, preserving the right side of the head with a clear view of the face. The eyes are rendered as two prominent rounded forms, with a chin below the mouth. One horn and one ear are broken. Rings around the horn are preserved, characteristic of the horned animal represented.
Lot, art, with no further object description or material information provided.

Figurine, limestone, depicting a seated human figure with broken legs, holding an animal-shaped object in both hands above a hole in the base and inserting a fuse into it; described as a sacrificed piece, probably from the 3rd century BC.

Statue, marble stone, human figure of a man standing on a rectangular two-level base, with both hands broken; the base bears a two-line blade line inscription. Dimensions: 380 x 160 mm. The object is dated to about 300 BCE to 100 BCE.

Gravestones, marble stone, in the form of human heads carved from marble in different colors, with long prominent noses, some broad, two prominent eyes, and small prominent mouths; three examples have inscriptions written in the pavilion line.

Human statue head, white marble, with a long neck encircled by three dark circular lines. The head has clear facial features, including apricot-shaped eyes with drilled holes for the missing pupils, eyebrows marked by horizontal lines, puffed cheeks, a puffed mouth, and prominent ears on both sides.
Object type and material cannot be determined from the provided text.
Table, marble stone, round-shaped, with a flat surface and standing on three cylindrical legs; the outer side is vertically marked with lines and shows traces of squid.
Bowl, bronze. Bronze bowl decorated with painted and/or incised motifs of three human figures, three palm trees, two plant motifs, and a snake. Dated to around the 4th to 2nd century BCE.

Saddle, bronze. Sacrificed object composed of an old oil-pouring tub fitted with a fuse holder; the end has a handle terminating at the front half of the oak, marked by the horns at the back, the chin below the mouth, and the feet extending forward.

Seal, black stone, rare cylindrical form, perforated for suspension, with a carved surface bearing symbols resembling an accelerator; dimensions 0.8 cm wide x 0.25 cm high (2 cm x 0.6 cm); dated to about 3400–3100 BCE.

Animal statue, bronze, depicting a camel with a high head and two upright ears, a collar around the neck, and a sleeve-like element over the back. Dated to around the 1st century BC to the 1st century AD.
Head of a human statue, marble stone, depicting a male head with clear facial features, closed eyes, a long nose, and a small mouth set in a straight horizontal line; the head has a short neck.

Stone vessel, marble, conical-shaped with a high crater and a base of larger diameter than the upper opening, without cover, with two knobs at the middle of the body, and showing white-coloured veining in the same stone.
Coins, silver. Old Yemeni coins, one face with an image of an owl and some with Latin letters, the other face likely showing a human face. Dated to around the fourth and third centuries BCE.
Lot, art, material not specified.
Sculptural fragments, marble stone: heads of human statues of different shapes and sizes, including one smashed and freckled, with a hint, together with a torso of a statue.

Inscription, bronze, 18 × 13 cm, framed; a bronze engraved inscription with a rusted surface, recorded as al-Silwi 3 and associated with the public confession engravings studied by Ibrahim Al-Silwi 3 (Salawi 3), based on an image shown by the late Khalil Al-Zubairi and published without a photograph in the Daci blog.

Coin, gold. Muslim Yemeni dinar, attributed to the reign of Ali Bin Mohammed Al-Salehi and described as belonging to the “right state”; found in Zebed. The coin bears an inscription in semi-Kufic script.
Coin, gold, Muslim Yemeni denar, dated to the reign of al-Sal, daughter of Ahmed al-Salehi, with a semi-Kufic inscription; reportedly found in Jabalah.
Lot, art, dimensions 14.2 × 23.8.

Hooves, bronze. A group of identical oversized bull hooves in bronze, each larger than normal size and larger than a large cube. Two examples have prominent holes above the cube for installation, and three examples are hollow inside, with holes at the back of the cubes and on the foot base.

Human statues, marble stone, lot of human statues carved in marble, similar in design, pose, and size, with one larger central statue distinguished by a pigeon held in the hand; the base bears a single-line inscription reading [A/T/R/R]. Dimensions: 10 × 5.5 × 3 × 7.25 × 3.25 × 1.5.

Human statues, marble stone, a group of carved marble figures comprising two seated statues, one standing figure beneath them, a bust, a small horse, and a mural with an ox’s head; dimensions recorded as 7 × 4 × 2.5 and 2.5 × 3 × 0.5.
Two human statues and a face, marble stone, comprising two human statues in a sitting position and an empty-eyed human mask with mouth and nose, all in marble stone.

Human bust, marble stone, depicting a woman with the hands extended forward, hair falling behind the ears, eyes with two recessed pupils, a long nose, and bars; the bust is placed on a separate inscribed base with a three-line inscription.
Tombstone, marble, with a human face carved in relief or incised on the stone, showing a long nose, eyebrows connected to the beard, two pierced and closed eyes, and a very small mouth.

Tombstone, marble stone, with a human face in relief or incised depiction showing a broad long rectangular nose, two visible eyes with indicated pupils, a small mouth, and a wide chin; below the face is a two-line inscription. The object is described as a marble tombstone and is dated to about 200 BCE.
Head, material not specified. The head of a human statue.

Human statue face, marble stone, carved in marble, with clear features; the eyes are blind, long and well connected, the eyebrows are strongly joined, the nostrils are open, the mouth is small, and the chin is broad. Dated to about 200 BC–100 BC.
Head of a human statue, marble stone, depicting a male head with a clear face, almond-shaped eyes, packed eyebrows, a long broken nose, closed lips, ears on both sides, and a short neck.
Camel statue, marble, standing on a rectangular base, with a pointed sleeve, a neck curved upward to the highest point, and the head emphasized to the front; dated to the 1st–2nd century BCE.

Gold ring, representing a seal, with an elliptical jet intaglio carved with the letters “N” and “D” on the left side and “A” and “M” on the right side; a sunbeam motif is also engraved at the top and bottom of the profile. Made of gold, dated to around the 2nd century AD. Weight 8.74 g.
Coin, silver, Dinar of Yemen, issued under Ali Ben Mohammed, dated 439-59/1047-67; weight 2.49 g; recorded as very soft silver.
Cup, bronze. Cylindrical cup with an inverted hole, decorated around the body with a four-line engraved inscription. Dated to the 4th–3rd centuries BC. Height 13.1 cm; diameter 7.5 cm.
Bronze engraved plate, bronze, rectangular in shape, with an inscription arranged in ten lines within a prominent framed border. The object measures 35.8 cm by 33.3 cm. Condition noted as good.

Lid, bronze. Oval-shaped lid with three feet in the form of lion’s feet; decorated with animal and vegetal motifs and a written inscription, with a single citrus-line border in the form of a tire at the edge.

Horse statue, bronze, depicting a horse standing on four legs with calves at the ends of the limbs; the mouth is open, and a rope is shown on the back, interpreted as indicating a harness for pulling a wagon. Bronze. 4th–3rd centuries BC. Height 12.1 cm; length 13.8 cm.

Bronze human statue in standing position, with the lower extremities scattered and the upper left extremities broken. The chest has a hollow cavity in the form of an inverted triangle, and the waist is encircled by a belt. Dated to the 1st century BC–1st century AD.

Head-shaped tombstone, stone: carved in Albister stone, depicting a human head in relief with hair falling from below the ears to the back of the head, triangular eyes, a straight nose, and a small closed mouth.

Human statue, marble stone, with clear features, standing on a base; both hands are extended forward and broken, and the lower limbs are set on a cubic-shaped base. The current condition of the piece is very good.

Tombstone, stone: carved funerary marker with a human head in relief, from Albister stone. The face is shown with lightly incised eyes, ears on both sides of the head, a straight nose, and a beard indicated below the mouth.

Statue, bronze. Camel statue with a passenger on its back, showing clear features; the rider is depicted holding a stone. The camel has a straight neck and a forward-facing head, rendered in motion. Dated to the 1st century BC.

Coin, metal, 4.85 g. Ancient Yemeni coin dated 354–340 BC, showing Greek influence. One face depicts an owl and a combination of two letters; the other face shows a human face with a pammelsand nose on the cheek.

Coin, metal. Ancient Yemeni coin showing Greek influence, with one face depicting an owl and a combination of two letters, and the other face depicting a human face with the nose shown on the cheek. Dated 354–340 BC. Weight 5.52 g.
Earring, gold, with an open knot of the same material, semi-circular in shape, decorated with mannequin-shaped elements and a crater carved with adjacent lines. 5th century BC. 2.5 × 2.2 cm.

Coin, metal, ancient Yemeni issue dated 354–340 BC, weighing 5.08 g. One face shows Greek influence, with an owl motif and a combination of two letters; the other side shows a human face, with a pammelsand nose on the cheek.

Coin, metal. Ancient Yemeni coin dated 354–340 BC, showing Greek influence. One face depicts an owl and a combination of two letters; the other face shows a human face with a pammelsand nose on the cheek.
Star-shaped stone objects, two crystals of pyrotechnic stone, each mediated by a high-altitude bump with an iron ring for suspension. Islamic period, Qatar. Dimensions: 18 cm and 12 cm.

Vase, copper. Bowl with a wide nozzle and a condensed base, with the outer body decorated with a book-tape motif and vegetal ornament. Dated to the 14th–15th century, Qatar. Height 27 cm. Very good condition.

Coin, metal, possibly silver, Islamic Yemeni currency with Arabic inscription in a surrounding written framework; the inscription reads “Sultan Muzaffar bin Yousif bin Al-Mama'b.” Dated 650 H (1252 CE). Weight 1.82 g. Condition noted as good.

Coin, metal, possibly silver, Islamic Yemeni currency with an Arabic inscription in a surrounding framework; the text includes “The God of Hila Muhammad ... [The Messenger of Hila].” Dated to the Apostolic period, 1249–1295, weight 1.91 g.

Cylinder box, wood, cylindrical in shape, with cover, decorated with plant and geometric decorative elements executed in prominent carved relief. 14th century. Height 16.2 cm; diameter 20.3 cm. Very good condition.

Coin, metal, possibly silver, Muslim Yemeni, with an Arabic inscription arranged within a four-tier framework; one face bears a legible inscription beginning “In the name of Allah al-Rahman al-Rahim...” and referring to Muhammad as رسول, while the other face is unclear. Dated to 696–721 AH. Weight 1.32 g.
Coin, metal, possibly silver. Muslim Yemeni coin surrounded by an Arabic written frame with unclear inscription. Apostolic State.

Coin, gold, a Muslim Yemeni two-faced coin dated 718 H, weighing 4.34 g. One face is surrounded by a four-tier Arabic inscription band and has a square frame containing an Arabic inscription reading “There is no god but God, Muhammad is the messenger of God”; the other face bears an Arabic inscription reading “Sultan King in Support [...] David.” The object is described as in excellent condition.
Plaque, bronze, engraved with seven lines of text on the surface; rectangular form, measuring 14 × 11 in. (36 × 28 cm); dated to the 1st–2nd century BC.

Head, marble, of a statue of a man, carved in a clear-cut style with two eyes, two eyebrows, and a mouth; dated to the 1st century B.C./1st century A.D. according to the source. The object is described as in good condition. Source attribution in the text mentions Hansokrams (1912–1992), Düsseldorf, Germany.

Statue, bronze, in the form of a camel. The figure is described as surrounding the top of the neck of a rope, with a tranquil inscription along the blade line that is unclear, and the body covered with a full layer of effort. Height 7.5 cm.

Statue, bronze, depicting a standing figure with one arm extended forward and the other extended to the side, mounted on a base; the body is covered by a rust layer that obscures details, including an unclear graft line. Dated to 4.2 BC. Height 11.5 cm.

Statue, bronze, of a standing man in motion, with a clear face, muzzle, hair, wrinkle, and sailor attributes as described in the source text. The object is recorded as missing. It is dated to the second century BCE and attributed to the southern Arabian Peninsula (old Yemen). Dimensions: 13.5 cm long.

Tombstone, marble, fragmentary, with incised inscription and relief decoration; described as a man’s tombstone, preserving part of the deceased’s name with the letter “N” visible in the final line, and featuring a human face motif with prominent eyes, a small mouth, and a strand or line beneath the mouth. Provenance is given as the southern Arabian Peninsula (old Yemen). Length 27.5 cm.

Statue head, marble: head of a male statue with a long neck, carved in marble; the face has high eyebrows, prominent eyes, a prominent nose, and a small mouth, with hair rendered in a clear, salty tombstone-like treatment.

Grave marker head, polished marble, in the form of a man’s head with a short neck, defined head, indicated hair, eyebrows, a prominent nose, and closed mouth, decorated with two lizards and two rosettes; from the Horn of the Arabian Peninsula (old Yemen); length 22 cm.
Statue, bronze, depicting two adjoining lizards connected at the tail and shown in motion. The object is almost 10 cm in diameter.

Palette, stone, with incised and carved decoration, 28 cm high and 23 cm wide, from the southern Arabian Peninsula (old Yemen), dated to the 2nd century AD. The object is described as a palette with drawings, showing sculptural decoration and a three-line inscription on the anthem line reading NFS, NBYR, BAT, BA, B.

Statue head, stone, of a male figure with a clearly modeled face and distinct eyes; carved in stone, from the southern Arabian Peninsula (ancient Yemen), dated to the 3rd century B.C.E., and measuring almost 20 cm in height.

Plaque, marble. Rectangular polished marble plaque with a base of the same stone, bearing five letters: “A click and HBL” and the inscription “Horn A and Miledi - Southern Arabian Peninsula (old Yemen).” Height 36 cm.

Tombstone, marble, cylindrical in shape, with a clear-cut surface, two perforations, and a small incised mouth; described as having a distinctive character in the making of gravestones. Origin: southern Arabian Peninsula (old Yemen). Height 18 cm. Missing.

Animal statue, stone. Statue of an animal on a rectangular base of the same material, with large horns visible; described as a “glimpse of big horns wrapped up.” Stone, 32.5 cm high. Dated to the 5th century B.C.E. and attributed to the southern Arabian Peninsula (old Yemen).

Object, bronze. Curved handle ending in an animal body with large horns curved backwards; two hands placed in front of a lumbar area bearing a three-letter inscription. Sacrificed. Dated to the 4th century B.C.E.

Tombstone, stone. Rectangular tombstone carved in low relief with a clear-cut discharge, featuring eyes, a prominent nose, and a semi-open mouth; inscribed with three letters reading p.P.R. Late 2000 BCE. Length 45.5 cm.
Tombstone, marble. Rectangular tombstone carved from a marble stone, from the Horn of the Arabian Peninsula (old Yemen). Length 38.2 cm.
Sword, polished metal and silver, with a blade of polished metal and a silver hilt or head decorated with silver filaments.

Metal artifact, metal, rectangular conglomerate on four legs, with edges decorated with geometric shapes; the sides are covered with a walrus-enveloped decorator and a restaurant with prominent animal head feces, and the inter-lettered emptiness includes various plant decorative elements.

Human statue, limestone, depicting two interconnected human figures in a similar salt-like material. The figures have prominent eyes and noses, heads joined by a small cylindrical bridge, and short hands held against the chest.

Human statue, sandstone, a woman’s bust with a visible head, large eyes inlaid with gypsum and cream stones, high eyebrows with stone inserts, a high sharp nose, full lips, hair arranged in lines and falling behind the neck, and two distinct ears.
Statue, marble, human figure represented as a woman’s bust with jealous eyes retaining traces of black fixing material, a straight nose, a small mouth, and curly hair falling behind the shoulders.
Human statue, material not specified. A human statue described in the source as “A human statue.”

Human statue, bronze, depicting a standing woman with a pronounced pregnant abdomen. The head is upright, with a gang and a clearly defined ellipse wrapped backwards and hanging behind the shoulders. The arms are extended outward and the hands are open. The garment covers the body to mid-thigh. The legs are clearly modelled, with distinct toes. Height: 13.2 cm. Date: toward the 2nd century BC.

Human statue, bronze, small flat-shaped figure representing a man in an abstract manner, standing in a long dress with both arms extended forward and holding a stick in the right hand; the figure is connected below to a small stabilization base. First millennium BC. Height: 13.7 cm.

Statuettes, bronze, four votive figurines representing human figures in an abstract manner, with unclear features; three are standing with outstretched, separate arms, and one is seated with the head missing; all are covered with a green layer of bronze corrosion. First millennium BC. Height: 2.2 to 4.2 cm.

Human statue fragment, bronze, preserving part of a hollow bronze figure: the left torso and shoulder, with a shield and a belt crossing the shoulders and extending to the back. First and second century C.E. Height: 21 cm.
Statue, bronze: human statue preserving the bronze palm of the right hand, rendered at natural size with long fingers and covered with a green layer of bronze corrosion. Length: 13 cm.

Statue, material not specified. Human statue depicting a woman, with a clearly rendered head and well-executed features. The eyes are oval, the eyebrows are arched and raised, the nose is prominent and sharp with a rounded end, and the mouth is represented by a curved line.
Statue head, marble, human figure, carved in stone. The object is described as the head of a statue, with a human form, made of marble.

Human statue, marble. Statue of a man carved in white marble, with a clearly shaped and sharply cut head. The face is described as gloomy, with semi-oval carved eyes, eyebrows above the eyes, a pierced nose, full and relaxed lips, and ears clearly indicated on both sides of the head and pierced. The hairstyle consists of short locks arranged in square and rectangular forms, and the neck is broad.

Human statue, bronze. Head of a bronze statue representing a young man with a clear gaze; the eyes are prominent, the nose is straight, and the mouth has full lips. The hair is arranged in strands ending in forward and side waves, and the head is crowned with a vegetal wreath surmounted by a decorative element.

Animal statue, marble, depicting a standing sheep with a clearly stylized form. The animal has large curved horns with spiral or snail-like terminals, a marked head, eyes not clearly represented, and a mouth indicated by a curved line. The body is cylindrical, and the statue stands on four legs. Dated to the second half of the 1st millennium BCE.

Animal statue, bronze, depicting a young male deer in a crouching position with the body turned to the right. The head is defined, with prominent ears and short horns. The body is rendered in a fluid manner, and the front legs are missing. The surface is covered with a layer of green bronze corrosion. Dated to the second half of the first millennium BC.

Tombstone, limestone. Rectangular grave marker with a human face in relief or carved depiction, showing raised eyebrows, rectangular nose, and a white mouth; described as “Jerry’s Stone.” Dated to the first century BCE.

Tombstone, limestone. Irregular grave marker carved in limestone, with a sculpted human face. The source text describes it as an irregular, semi-rivaled grave of limestone and notes that the face was sculpted on a human face; no further details are provided.
Object, material not specified, with dimensions 3.5 x 25.5.
Object type not specified, material not specified. The source text only gives the name “PERRE BERGE & Associes” and does not provide any archaeological or object-specific information.

Tablet, bronze, with an inscription and window holes distributed on three angles for stabilization. The engraved text records a vow made by a person named Saad El for the Abed, who was tried at Shebhan’s temple, invoking the gods Athtar, uncle, and prophet; the vow was made on his behalf and on behalf of his brother and his son. Dimensions recorded as 31.5 x 26.6 cm.
Object type and material cannot be determined from the provided text, which appears to contain only a partial inscription or catalog notation with no descriptive information about the object itself.

Rectangular plate of limestone, carved in low relief, depicting men wearing robes with carefully rendered folds; one figure is shown holding a saddle while another sits behind, both with head coverings framing a partial view of the face. The scene is arranged above a pin line and includes two lines of inscription. The source text also mentions marble and refers to the beginning of the Elephants of the Miles.
Painting, limestone. A painting made of limestone.

Plaque, bronze, rectangular plaque with a portrait composition in relief, showing three heads in frontal view, described as oak or bull heads, each with a prominent sculptural feature and a vegetal element above the head; the central head is framed by an engraved line and accompanied by an inscription of four characters reading “Bronze.”

Marble, rectangular object with a square section, carved with two deep cavities on the upper surface with raised rims. One rectangular face bears a one-line rectangular inscription in the strand line, above which is a decorative frame of chain triangles; a distinct mass of stone remains on one side. The inscription reads: (WPB/LTN/RAAA/RAAN/W). Marble. Second half of the first millennium BC.

Architectural component, bronze: round disk mediated by an eight-pointed star enclosed in a circle, surrounded by a frame of twelve bulls with snail motifs. End of the first millennium BC. Diameter 15 cm.

Seal, gold. Circular seal with an empty-drilled vent; the face is mediated by an inscription in a rectangular frame, and from top to bottom a dam is carved in reverse motion position. The inscription reads: (m/pn/).

Necklace, gold, formed as a spiraled wire with a central pendant representing the head of a hollow bull with short horns; on either side are two beads of planned faith, separated from the pendant by gold rings. Third century BC. Length: 5.15 cm.

Altar, marble. Cube-shaped altar flattened from the top of a bull’s head, with a clearly stylized form and carved inscription on the edge reading HBL/ML/SL/CTFR. Dated to the 5th–4th centuries BCE. Height: 28.2 cm; width: 8.2 cm.

Mural, marble, depicting two winged sheep separated by a prominent tree and marked by a band of semi-oval decorative forms; dated to around the 1st century B.C. to the 1st century A.D.; width 15 7/8 in. (40.3 cm).
Statue, marble. Statue of a standing man with both hands extended toward the ground, standing on a square base with an incised pavilion line. Dated to around the 2nd century. Height 30 cm.

Pendant, gold. Bull-shaped pendant with a perforated hole in the middle, showing the rope coils around the neck and a prominent decorative element on the front. Dated to around the 1st century BC to the 1st century AD. Weight 9.20 g; height 3.3 cm; height 3 cm.
Camel statue, marble, depicting a camel in clearly carved marble with a prominent sleeve and an indistinct head, standing on a rectangular base. Dated to around the 3rd century BCE.
Human figurine, mud, small mud doll with a clear head and an unclear body. Prehistoric. Height 120 millimeters.
Glass, bronze, with a suffocating form covering the fat, decorated with a prominent sculptural inscription in skeleton line composed of four lines. Height 10.5 cm. Dated to the 3rd–2nd century BCE.

Bust, bronze, of a man wearing a helmet, with a clearly modeled face, straight mouth, and shoulders indicated by a maple-like ligament; the lower part is missing. Height 14 cm. Dated to about the 1st century BCE.
Scorpion, bronze, clearly salty, with four legs, the fourth broken, and a body ending in an upraised tail. Around the 3rd–2nd century B.C.E. Length 10 cm; weight 118.9 g.

Tombstone, marble. White marble tombstone carved with a male face in relief, showing clear eyes, possibly with two stone eyebrows above them, a straight line for the brows, a prominent nose, a protruding mouth, and upper lips with holes representing a moustache and chin. Dated to the 3rd–2nd century BCE.

Tombstone, stone, rectangular, with a sculpted human face in relief showing the eyes, nose, and mouth aligned in a straight line; the lower part bears an inscription in blade line with a third letter, possibly the name of the deceased. Dimensions: 11 x 28 cm. Dated to the 3rd to 1st century BCE. Condition: good.

Mural, marble, with decorative relief elements including vegetative, animal, and architectural motifs. The composition features two imaginary windows formed by three overlapping rectangles rising from the right side, a sculpted bull on the head of a corn leaf, and on the left side a two-letter pile of an accelerator (and d) with a prominent long-horned bull extending to both sides. Around 500 B.C. Dimensions: 17 cm x 16.5 cm.

Statue, material not specified, depicting a horse with a rider seated on its back. The horse is shown with clear features, one foreleg raised in a parade stance. The rider is represented wearing clothes, with a sword at the waist; one hand is broken and missing.
Necklace, material not specified, in bull shape.

Necklace, gold, in the form of a statue of a naked, winged woman. The object is made of gold of a unique kind and is described as being dominated by an art style characterized by a complex composition.
Candlestick, copper alloy, large candlestick of sect form, commissioned by a man of the legal tiles of Solomon, Turkey, Ottoman. Dated to the month of Al-Hajjay 945 AH / May 1539 CE. Height 47.5 cm.

Human statue, marble stone, small, standing on a thin rectangular base, with the hands extended forward and broken, and with empty eyes filled with cream stone. Dimensions: 13 3/8 × 4 3/4 × 4 5/16 in. (34 × 12 × 11 cm). Dated to the 2nd century BCE or 1st century CE.
Tombstone, marble, with a rectangular base and a single-line engraved inscription; polished marble stone. Dimensions: 5 7/8 x 3 15/16 x 3 5/16 in. (15 x 10 x 8.4 cm). Mid-2nd century.

Human statue head, marble stone, with a long neck encircled by three drifting lines; the face is partially preserved, with a broken nose and Lusitanian eyes. Manuscript, paper, Hebrew and Arabic, Yemen, 19th century. The page describes three Yemeni manuscripts listed under this number at auction: a Hebrew manuscript with instructions and comments in the margins; a manuscript with Arabic instructions and a colophon on the last page; and a manuscript described as an Easter text with a Jewish translation into Arabic. All are on paper and dated to the 19th century.

Hebrew manuscript on paper, dated 1848, comprising 227 leaves and written in two columns per page with red ink headings and dialects. The manuscript includes red and black decorative elements at the beginning of each Nir, except Nirs VI and VII, and a framed title page (blonin) bearing the inscription that the book was written in Sana'a in 1848. Copies of the writer appear at the end of the book. It is described as a complete manuscript of formation, lacking a number of index leaves, and predating the well-known Livorno edition. Condition is noted as good.

Object, material not specified. The source text contains only fragmentary and repetitive bibliographic-style references, including the years 1895, 1894, 1934, 1986, and the notation “25 + 9 kedem [133],” but provides no identifiable object type, material, form, technique, dimensions, or period.

Manuscript, paper, Hebrew, 19th century. A bound manuscript containing a gathering of manuscripts and holy books from Yemen, including Third Megillot (Song of Songs, Ruth and Ecclesiastes) with commentary by Talmud, Rashi, and later combined commentaries such as Mahary Tsalakh, as well as Parashat Peshit’s papers with an adult explanation; the volume includes material at the beginning and end of the manuscript.

Hebrew manuscript on paper, two Yemeni manuscripts wrapped in the original skin, comprising liturgical and devotional texts. The first manuscript contains Piyyutim of Bakashot, Tikun Chatzot, interpretation of tekufot, shtarot, “Chiddushin,” Birkat Hamazon with Chiddushin, a eulogy, and a set of comments; it is dated 1864, includes different owner signatures, comprises 131 leaves, measures approximately 16.5 cm, and is in fair condition with bleeding, tears, and stains, with an old leather binding and popular decoration. The second manuscript contains piyyutim of Selichot and Hosha’anot, is dated to the 19th/20th century in Yemen, comprises approximately 168 leaves, measures about 16.5 cm, and is in good condition.

Manuscript, paper; Hebrew manuscript for liturgical use throughout the year, including weekdays, Saturday, and festivals, in Yemeni writing in an animated letter, dated to the 19th century and measuring 17 cm. The manuscript contains a title page with the text “Seder of the hall according to the Safari tradition, written throughout the year...” and, on the back of the title page, “A copy of the lounge and the lounge according to our traditions in prayers throughout Yemeni cities.” It includes a weekly and Saturday service, Rosh Chodesh service, Birkat HaLevana, Seder Lashanah, Tekun Burim, Tekun Besash with Al-Hajdah, prayers for the three festivals, and the Zikhrot for Shaft. Some leaves were later replaced by another hand; several leaves are missing. The manuscript is in separate leaves, unbound, with spots, holes, tears affecting the text of several leaves, and engravings.
